does: LeakAgent develops a reinforcement learning-based red-teaming framework that automatically generates adversarial prompts to extract private information from large language models. The system trains an open-source LLM as an attack agent to craft prompts that elicit both training data and system prompt leakage. The paper introduces a novel reward function providing fine-grained feedback on attack success, alongside mechanisms balancing exploration and exploitation during RL training and promoting diversity in generated adversarial prompts. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that LeakAgent substantially outperforms existing rule-based and manual red-teaming approaches for extracting training data, achieving higher success rates in automated privacy breach discovery across different model architectures and configurations.
connects: This work builds on emerging research documenting privacy vulnerabilities in LLMs, following studies showing these models can leak training data, system prompts, and personal information under adversarial prompting. It extends prior red-teaming methodologies by moving beyond manual prompt engineering and narrowly-focused system prompt extraction techniques. The paper combines reinforcement learning for adversarial attack generation with reward shaping, drawing from security research on automated vulnerability discovery and black-box attack optimization. It responds to the limitation that existing approaches fail to comprehensively address training data leakage risks, which recent work has identified as a severe and underexplored privacy threat in deployed LLM systems.
enables: LeakAgent establishes automated frameworks for comprehensively stress-testing LLM privacy defenses before deployment. The RL-based approach could enable development of more robust defense mechanisms by systematically identifying previously unknown vulnerability patterns in privacy-critical systems. This work points toward adversarial training pipelines where models learn to resist learned attack strategies, creating dynamic red-teaming loops. Future research could extend the framework to multi-turn attack sequences, adaptive defenses against RL-based attackers, and theoretical characterization of privacy leakage surfaces. The automated attack generation methodology may inform privacy-preserving training techniques and inform policy discussions around responsible LLM deployment and evaluation standards.
